headline: The Hidden Deadlines: How Long Do You Have to Report a Work Injury in Utah and Idaho?
articleBody:
Hidden Deadlines: Reporting Your Work Injury in Utah and Idaho

Time is the enemy of any workers' compensation claim. While the law provides time for you to recover, it also sets strict deadlines for when you must tell your boss about an injury. Failing to meet these dates can result in a permanent loss of benefits, regardless of how badly you were hurt.

The 'Notice' Deadline vs. the 'Filing' Deadline

There are two distinct clocks you must watch. First is the notice deadline—telling your employer you were hurt. In Utah, you should report the injury immediately, but the law technically gives you 180 days. However, waiting this long almost always leads to a denial. In Idaho, you generally have 60 days to give notice. The second clock is the claims deadline, which is the time you have to file a formal application for a hearing with the state if your claim is denied (typically several years).

Why You Should Report Within 24 Hours

Even though the law gives you weeks or months, reporting within 24 hours is the best way to protect your claim. Insurance companies are suspicious of 'Monday morning reports'—injuries that supposedly happened Friday but weren't mentioned until the following week. They will argue you got hurt over the weekend. Reporting immediately, with witnesses if possible, eliminates this defense.

Exceptions for Occupational Illness

The rules change for illnesses like lung disease or carpal tunnel. Because these don't happen in a single moment, the clock begins when you are aware of the injury and its connection to work. If a doctor in Salt Lake City tells you that your breathing issues are linked to the chemicals at your plant, your reporting period begins that day.

What if I Missed the Deadline?

If you have missed a deadline, do not give up immediately. There are limited exceptions if the employer already knew about the injury or if there was a valid reason for the delay. An attorney at Just Work Comp Law can review your specific timeline and help determine if an exception applies to your case. Call us at 801-746-0290 for a free consultation.
